coredata.dc:title: Temperature triggered high-performance carbon dots with robust solvatochromic effect and self-quenching-resistant deep red solid state fluorescence for specific lipid droplet imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Dong
Liu, Amin
Zhang, Yusheng
Zhu, Yuda
Wei, Dan
Sun, Jing
Luo, Hongrong
Fan, Hongsong
coredata.dc:description: Developing high-performance carbon dots (CDs) with new structure and establishing the linkage with synthetic conditions remain challenging. In this work, we develop a simple one-pot solvothermal strategy to synthesize high-performance CDs by rationally controlling carbonized core and polymer structure. The obtained CDs not only show robust solvatochromic effect with emission shift up to ~132 nm from green to far-red region meanwhile accompanied with quantum yield (QY) decreasing from 73 to 1% as solvent polarity increase, but also possess rare deep red self-quenching-resistant solid state fluorescence (λem = 660 nm). Extensive characterizations reveal that the thermal-triggered unique polymer features composed of carbonized core and the shell of intertwined polymer chains account for the unprecedented photophysical behaviors. Moreover, the CDs display ultrabright yellow emission in oil media (QY up to 51.6%) with an impressive fluorescence enhancement up to 140-fold compared to aqueous media, making them an efficient probe for specifically staining lipid droplets (LDs). In contrast with commercially LDs marker BODIPY 493/503, the CDs-based probe can provide long emission, large Stokes shifts (>100 nm), superior photostability, and especially overcome aggregation-caused-quenching. Consequently, the CDs-based marker successfully achieves real-time monitoring of LDs-related physiological process in living cells, diagnosing fatty liver and visualizing lipid-rich tissues in zebrafish. We believe the developed CDs-based LDs probe is more competitive against organic dyes due to their high efficiency, simple one-pot preparation and low-cost.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of the unusual multiple-color emissive CDs. 3D FL spectroscopy analysis and PL emission spectra of thermal temperature triggered multiple-color emissive CDs: (b and g) 120 °C, (c and h) 150 °C, (d and i) 170 °C, (e and j) 190 °C, (f and k) 210 °C, respectively. Inset: photographs of the multiple-color emissive CDs under UV light (365 nm). All these optical spectra were measured in DMF.

3D FL spectroscopy analysis of the representative green fractions and red fractions (a) G-CDs120, (b) G-CDs210, (c) R-CDs210. (d) Normalized PL emission spectra, (e) UV–vis absorption spectra and (f) PL excitation spectra of the five separated green fractions. (g) Normalized PL emission spectra, (h) UV–vis absorption spectra and (i) PL excitation spectra of the separated red fractions. All these optical spectra were measured in DMF.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and corresponding diameter distribution histogram of (a) G-CDs120, (b) G-CDs210 and (c) R-CDs210. (d) FT-IR spectra and (e) XPS spectra of of G-CDs120 (black curve), G-CDs210 (blue curve) and R-CDs (red curve). High-resolution XPS C1s spectra of (f) G-CDs120, (g) G-CDs210, (h) R-CDs210 and N1s spectra of the (i) G-CDs120, (j) G-CDs210, and (k) R-CDs210.

(a) The relationship between viscosity and shear rate for the three CDs at 20 °C. (b) The relationship between viscosity and temperature for the three CDs when shear rate is 10 s−1. (e) A possible growth mechanism from G-CDs120 to G-CDs210 and R-CDs210.

(a) The photographs of the three selected CDs under daylight (Top) and 365 nm UV light (Down). (b) PL emission spectrum of the R-CDs210 powder. Normalized UV–vis absorption (c) and (d) PL spectra, and (e) PL intensity of the R-CDs210 in various organic solvents with the concentration of 0.01 mg mL−1. (f) Photographs of the R-CDs210 in different solvents taken under 365 nm UV irradiation.

(a) Spectral overlap of dilute aqueous solution of R-CDs210 (0.01 mg mL −1). (b) Fluorescence decay curves of R-CDs210 aqueous solution (0.01 mg mL −1) and powders. (c) Schematic of self-quenching-resistance of R-CDs210 power. (d) The Fluorescence decay curves of R-CDs210 in the selected solvents monitored at their optimal emission wavelengths. (e) The Fluorescence decay curves of R-CDs210 in DMF under excitation wavelength of 455 nm. (f) TRES of R-CDs210 in DMF under excitation wavelength of 455 nm. (g) Two-dimensional pseudocolor map of TA spectra of R-CDs210 expressed in ΔOD (the change of the absorption intensity of the sample after excitation) as a function of both delay time and probe wavelength excited at 365 nm. (h) TA spectra of R-CDs210 at indicated delay times from 0.5 ps to 5 ns. (i) Global fitting results of R-CDs210 with four exponent decay functions.

Normalized UV absorption (a) and emission spectra (b) of R-CDs210 in PBS buffer and in oil (triglyceride) showing the impressive fluorogenic effect. Concentration of R-CDs210 was 10 μg/mL. Excitation wavelength was 480 nm. (c) Co-localization images of HepG2 cells stained with R-CDs210 (10 μg mL−1) for 1 h and subsequently with BODIPY493/503 (1 μg mL−1) for 10 min. (c1) Bright field. (c2) Yellow fluorescence of R-CDs210. (c3) Red pseudocolor fluorescence of BODIPY 493/503. (c4) Merged images of BODIPY 493/503 signal and R-CDs210 signals. (c5) A scatter plot indicating a correction coefficient of (c2) and (c3). (c6) Three-dimensional image of R-CDs210 (yellow fluorescence) and BODIPY 493/503 (Red pseudocolor fluorescence). R-CDs210: λe = 405 nm. BODIPY 493/503: λex = 488 nm. Scale bar: 10 μm. (d) Photostability of R-CDs210 and BODIPY493/503 in HepG2 cells under continuous irradiation. Irradiation conditions: for R-CDs210: 405 nm laser, laser power = 30%; for BODIPY493/503: 488 nm laser, laser power = 30%. Scale bar: 10 μm. (e) Multicolor imaging of HepG2 cells using R-CDs210 as a yellow emitting LDs marker. R-CDs210 (10 μg mL−1, λex = 488 nm), DAPI (5 μg mL−1, λex = 405 nm), CM-Dil (2 μg mL−1, λex = 543 nm). Scale bar: 10 μm. (f) Confocal images of HepG2 cells cultured in HBSS for 0 h (control), 2 h, 4 h and 8 h, followed by staining with R-CDs210 (10 μg mL−1, 1 h, λex = 488 nm) and MitoTracker Red (2 μM, λex = 543 nm), respectively. Scale bar: 10 μm.

(a) Confocal images of liver tissues from general-feeding mice and high-fat feeding mice stained with R-CDs210 (10 μg mL−1, λex = 488 nm) as well as their corresponding magnified images in white rectangular areas; the red arrows point to LD clusters formed in liver tissues of high-fat feeding mice. (b) Confocal fluorescent images of zebrafish stained with R-CDs210 (10 μg mL−1, λex = 488 nm) and BODIPY 493/503 ((1 μg mL−1, λex = 488 nm). Scale bar: 500 μm.

head.ce:title: Temperature triggered high-performance carbon dots with robust solvatochromic effect and self-quenching-resistant deep red solid state fluorescence for specific lipid droplet imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing high-performance carbon dots (CDs) with new structure and establishing the linkage with synthetic conditions remain challenging. In this work, we develop a simple one-pot solvothermal strategy to synthesize high-performance CDs by rationally controlling carbonized core and polymer structure. The obtained CDs not only show robust solvatochromic effect with emission shift up to ~132 nm from green to far-red region meanwhile accompanied with quantum yield (QY) decreasing from 73 to 1% as solvent polarity increase, but also possess rare deep red self-quenching-resistant solid state fluorescence (λem = 660 nm). Extensive characterizations reveal that the thermal-triggered unique polymer features composed of carbonized core and the shell of intertwined polymer chains account for the unprecedented photophysical behaviors. Moreover, the CDs display ultrabright yellow emission in oil media (QY up to 51.6%) with an impressive fluorescence enhancement up to 140-fold compared to aqueous media, making them an efficient probe for specifically staining lipid droplets (LDs). In contrast with commercially LDs marker BODIPY 493/503, the CDs-based probe can provide long emission, large Stokes shifts (>100 nm), superior photostability, and especially overcome aggregation-caused-quenching. Consequently, the CDs-based marker successfully achieves real-time monitoring of LDs-related physiological process in living cells, diagnosing fatty liver and visualizing lipid-rich tissues in zebrafish. We believe the developed CDs-based LDs probe is more competitive against organic dyes due to their high efficiency, simple one-pot preparation and low-cost.

ce:section.ce:para: Scientific interest in carbon dots (CDs) have continued to increase over the past few years due to their numerous merits including simple synthesis procedure, low-cost sources, outstanding optical properties and excellent biocompatibility [1–5], which have triggered intensive researches to achieve their wide potential applications in bioimaging, sensing, light-emitting diode (LED), and more [6–10]. For practical applications, high photoluminescence (PL) quantum yield (QY) is of great significance. To date, CDs with QY up to 90% have been widely achieved in blue and green region [11,12]. However, few attempts have succeeded in highly efficient long-wavelength emissive CDs with high QY, restricting further applications, especially in bioimaging. On the other hand, although CDs with deep red or near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence have been realized in solution [13], they fail to realize same PL behavior in solid state due to the aggregation-caused-quenching (ACQ) effect [14,15]. Embedding CDs in solid matrices such as polymer and silica xerogel is an efficient strategy to maintain solid-state-fluorescence (SSF) by preventing CD particles from direct contact [16]. Unfortunately, these efforts have not yet addressed ACQ in aggregate due to the restriction of doped concentration. So far, only a handful of examples involving CDs powder with inherent SSF from blue to green lights have been achieved via grafting polymer chains into CDs [17,18], but red or deep red SSF CDs have not been conquered yet.

Dyes with solvatochromic effect possess high sensitivity to environmental polarity by alterations in both emission peak and PL intensity [19], making them potential fluorescent probes for monitoring intracellular polarity and lipid structures since these parameters are particularly vital for maintaining normal physiological functions in living cells [20,21]. Current solvatochromic dyes mainly focus on organic compounds and aggregation induced emission (AIE) luminogens, which suffer from some critical challenges such as complication and high-cost for preparation [20,22]. The frequently employed structural motifs for solvatochromic dyes is a donor-bridge-acceptor (D-π-A) system, in which the D-A unit provides intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) character while π conjugation system can contribute to long wavelength emission [23]. Resorting to common sense, the representative structure of CDs consists of carbonized core with π conjugated system and different kinds of functional groups including electron-donating groups and electron-accepting groups on the surface [24,25]. Therefore, CDs theoretically can form D-π-A unit, possibly yielding solvatochromic effect in long wavelength region. Unfortunately, although CDs with solvent-dependent emission have been reported for several times [26,27], none of them have achieved truly solvatochromic effect with large red-shifted fluorescence and sharply decreased PL intensity and QY upon the increase of solvent polarity. Consequently, it is highly desired to develop CDs with robust solvatochromic effect and explore the related mechanisms, making them more competitive against organic molecules, QDs and AIE luminogens.

It is widely accepted that controlling the structure of CDs is the key to adjust their properties. During a typical solvo/hydrothermal process, the CDs would undergo dehydration, polymerization, aromatization and carbonization processes [11,28,29]. Therefore, by rationally controlling the thermal temperature, CDs with different structure and composition varying from complete polymer structure, carbogenic core with sp2-hybridized domains embedded in an amorphous sp3-hybridized matrix and significant graphitized structure with obvious carbon lattice can be realized. Among the three typical structure models of CDs, carbogenic core with sp2-hybridized domains embedded in an amorphous sp3-hybridized matrix is most likely to generate D-π-A unit because the carbogenic core with sp2-hybridized domains can serve as π structure while amorphous sp3-hybridized matrix can provide electron-donating and electron-accepting groups. In addition, the PL properties of CDs formed at relatively low temperature are dominant by molecular chromophore, thus showing high QY [30,31]. Once experienced high temperature, these molecular chromophore undergo further carbonization and the resulting carbonized core state govern the optical performance with low QY [11]. Inspired by the relationship between CDs’ structure and temperature as well as the requirements for producing SSF, solvatochromic effect and high QY, we put forward that it’s possible to control the formation of CDs with different structure by regulating the temperature, giving rise to CDs with desired polymer chains and special D-π-A structural unit.

Herein, a facile synthetic strategy is developed to prepare high-performance CDs by tuning thermal temperature during solvothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and thiourea (Fig. 1 a). We show that the thermal temperature can efficiently modulate the structures and optical properties of prepared CDs and as expected, high-performance CDs with robust solvatochromic effect (~132 nm shift), deep red SSF (λem = 660 nm), and high QY up to 73% are successfully synthesized. Detailed characterizations reveal that the unique polymer characteristics, composed of carbonized core with efficient π conjugated system and the shells of intertwined polymer chains, which, together with different functional groups on the surface, are proposed to account for the impressive optical properties of the CDs. More importantly, due to the unique solvatochromic effect, the obtained CDs are demonstrated capable of serving as superior LDs-specific fluorescent probe in both vitro and vivo with favorable QY (51.6%), large Stokes shifts (>100 nm), good photostability and excellent biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and purification of temperature triggered multiple-color emissive CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized by one-pot solvothermal method of heating o-phenylenediamine and thiourea in the mixed solvents of DMF and water. As the thermal temperature gradually increased from 120 to 210 °C, the resulting CDs solutions displayed tunable fluorescence from green to red, under 365 nm UV light irradiation (Fig. 1a and Fig. S1d). Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescent (FL) spectroscopy analysis was carried out to preliminarily explore the nature of the temperature triggered multiple-color emissions. Interestingly, only two excitation-independent PL peaks were observed, centered at 525 and 600 nm (Fig. 1b–k), respectively. Subsequently, the prepared CDs were strictly purified via silica gel column chromatography. For samples formed at relatively low temperature (120 °C and 150 °C), only green emissive CDs with excitation-independent PL peaks at 525 nm can be separated (Fig. S2). When thermal temperature exceeded 170 °C, two main fluorescent components were successfully separated, exhibiting excitation-independent green emission and red emission, respectively (Figs. S3–S5). These results are in consistent with the data of 3D FL spectra. In principle, yellow and orange can be realized by combining green with red. Therefore, the visual multiple-color emissions are demonstrated actually a dynamic combination of green- and red-emissive CDs with different proportions (Fig. 1a).

The optical properties of these isolated fractions were investigated to reveal the effects of thermal temperature on their PL behaviors. The five separated green fractions all exhibit excitation-independent emission with emission peak located at around 525 nm (Fig. 2 a, b and d; Figs. S2–S5). In the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2e), the peak at 270 nm, corresponding to π-π* transitions of the sp2-hybridized core [32,33], emerges as a small shoulder for the low temperature (120 °C) samples, maximizing in intensity when the temperature exceeds 190 °C, indicating the increase of carbonization degree. In addition, the broad peak in the range of 400–450 nm gradually decreases in intensity as the temperature increases while another peak at 380 nm arises for the samples from 190 °C onward. In the PL excitation spectra, the optimal excitation wavelengths shift from 420 to 380 nm for the 190 and 210 °C samples (Fig. 2f), with positions overlapped well with corresponding absorption bands, suggesting the alteration of emission centers from surface sub-fluorophores to edge state of carbon core [34,35]. In contrast, the thermal temperatures (170 to 210 °C) show negligible effect on the optical behavior of red fractions except for slight difference in carbonization levels (Fig. 2g–i). The optimal excitation wavelengths of these red fractions are around 480 nm, corresponding to the surface or molecular state emission [34,36]. This feature is quite different from the green fractions obtained at same temperature (i.e., G-CDs190 and G-CDs210), in which edge state of carbon core is dominant. These results reveal that high temperature can not only promote the carbonization degree, but also contribute to red-shifted fluorescence [16].

The above results suggest a possible transformation between green CDs and red CDs. In order to confirm this hypothesis, we designed an experiment by solvothermal treatment of G-CDs120 at 210 °C. As shown in Fig. S6, two fluorescent fractions with optical properties resembling those of G-CDs210 and R-CDs210 were successfully separated, directly confirming that G-CDs120 can act as seeds and further grow into G-CDs210 and R-CDs210.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature triggered three typical CDs show varied structure and composition
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the transformation of CDs with temperature as well as the red-shifted fluorescence, three representative CDs, G-CDs120, G-CDs210 and R-CDs210 were further characterized. We firstly investigated their morphology by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) image. As is clearly seen, G-CDs120 display relatively large polymeric structure without any dot-shape nanoparticles (Fig. 3 a). The diameter of these polymeric structure varies from 15 to 35 nm with average size determined to be 25 nm. G-CDs210 present contracted small particles with mean size of about 12 nm, and meanwhile, some polymer-like structure can still be observed (Fig. 3b). R-CDs210 exhibit well dispersed dot-shape nanoparticles with average diameter of 10 nm (Fig. 3c). The decreased sizes from G-CDs120 to R-CDs210 are further confirmed by AFM images, with the thickness determined to be 20–35 nm, 8–14 nm and 5–10 nm for G-CDs120, G-CDs210 and R-CDs210, respectively (Fig. S7). The variations of size and morphology from polymeric structure to dot-shape nanoparticles are in consistence with the formation process of most reported CDs as thermal temperature increases [28,37]. Generally, due to the degradation of polymer chains under strong electrical field and low contrast of polymer structures, the high-resolution TEM images mainly record the morphology of carbon core and the polymer structures are always difficult to be obtained. As a result, although the high-resolution TEM images reveal no clear lattice fringes for these CDs (Fig. S8), a gradually increased contrast from G-CDs120 to R-CDs210 is observed, corresponding to the transformation from polymeric structure to carbonized core structure. In the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. S9), the three CDs are featured by a series of sharp diffraction peaks, which is different from the typical form characterized by a broad peak around 26° but resemble that of some reported carbonized polymer dots with polymer structures [38,39].

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were employed to explore the chemical structures and compositions of the three selected CDs. FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3d) uncover the stretching vibration for O–H (3380–3436 cm−1), C–H (2920 cm−1), CO/CN (1600–1650 cm−1), CC (1469–1500 cm−1), C–N (1415–1440 cm−1), C–O–C (1081 cm−1) for the three CDs [30,39], suggesting their similar surface functional groups and chemical bonds. However, thermal temperature greatly alters the content of these groups and meanwhile yields some new groups. For example, G-CDs120 show more significant peaks for N–H (3200–3260 cm−1), CO/CN, C–N and C–O–C bonds, confirming their polymer-like structure. CC and C–OH bonds are more pronounced in R-CDs210, suggesting the relatively high carbonization degree. A specific peak centered at 2055 cm−1 appears in G-CDs210 alone, which should be assigned to -SCN bond. The full survey of XPS displayed in Fig. 3e show that the three CDs all consist of C, N, O, and S elements. The atomic ratio of C element is determined to be 67.62%, 73.74% and 84.53% for G-CDS120, G-CDs210 and R-CDs210, respectively, demonstrating the increase of carbonization degree. Fig. 3f–k illustrate the high-resolution XPS C1s and N1s spectra. The high-resolution C1s spectra can be fitted into four different types of C species: C–C/CC, C–N, C–O, and CO. The N1s spectra show three peaks at 398.2, 399.1, 400.2 eV, which are assigned to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively. The O1s spectra can be deconvoluted into two peaks associated with C–O and CO (Fig. S10a–c), and the S2p spectra feature several peaks of S–C, S–H, S–S and SO (Fig. S10d–f). The high-resolution spectra suggest similar chemical groups in the three CDs, but the contents of these groups are different, consistent with the result of FTIR. The contents of these chemical bonds are illustrated in Fig. S11. Interestingly, the amount of N- and O-related chemical bonds in G-CDs120 and R-CDs210 are extremely close while the only difference is that the CC/C–C bond dramatically increase from G-CDs120 to R-CDs210, implying that the surface of R-CDs210 resembles that of G-CDs120 expect the content of sp2 carbon. In addition, compared with G-CDs120 and R-CDs210, G-CDs210 possess higher amount of C-O, pyridinic N and graphitic N, suggesting significant alteration of their structure. These results manifest that although both G-CDs210 and R-CDs210 stem from G-CDs120, they undergo different growth process.

Subsequently, NMR spectra (1H and 13C) were taken to distinguish the sp3-hybridized carbon from the sp2-hybridized carbon. The 1H NMR spectra display obvious aromatic hydrogen signals in the range of 7.5–8.5 ppm for G-CDs210 and R-CDs210 (Fig. S12) [40]. Moreover, in the 13C NMR spectra (Fig. S13), G-CDs210 and R-CDs210 give rise to apparent signals in the range of 110–140 ppm, which are indicative of the sp2 C atoms [41]. In contrast, for G-CDs120, the signals in the range of 7.5–8.5 ppm are weak while no signals can be observed in the range of 110–140 ppm, confirming that almost no carbonized structure is formed in G-CDs120. Subsequently, the polymer features of the three CDs were identified by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and the derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) [30]. All the three CDs display a typical one-step decomposition process, resemble that of polymers (Fig. S14a). The weight loss of G-CDs210 and R-CDs210 before 200 °C should be ascribed to the removal of physically adsorbed water [42]. The DTG curve is used to estimate the weight loss rate (Fig. S14b). For G-CDs210 and R-CDs210, almost no weight loss process happens when the temperature is higher than 400 °C, and the final residual weights are around 7% for the two CDs, representing the weight percentage of carbon cores [30]. However, G-CDs120 still lose weight at a slow rate over 400 °C, which should be ascribed to the degradation of the cross-linked polymer network. When the temperature exceeds 600 °C, the degradation rate becomes faster and finally G-CDs120 almost completely degrade without residual. The polymer structures of the three CDs were further tested by viscosity characteristics. As shown in Fig. 4 a, both G-CDs120 and R-CDs210 show shear thinning behavior at 20 °C. Besides, the viscosity of the two CDs decreases as the temperature gradually increases from 20 to 60 °C (Fig. 4b and d) [30]. It is worth noting that although G-CDs210 fail to display shear thinning behavior, the viscosity decreases with increase in the temperature (Fig. 4c), resembling a typical response of the polymer to the temperature [43,44]. The results of TGA, DTG and viscosity indicate that the G-CDs210 and R-CDs210 possess unique polymer characteristics of carbonized cores and the shell of polymer chains, while G-CDs120 are complete polymer structure.

Taken together, we propose a mechanism for the evolution of structure and red-shifted fluorescence of CDs as temperature increases (Fig. 4e). At low temperature, o-PD and thiourea undergo dehydration and polymerization reaction to form polymeric structures consisting of intertwined polymer chains and crosslinked polymer network. No carbonized structure or efficient π conjugated system is formed in G-CDs120 and some special groups, namely sub-fluorophores or crosslinked polymer chains dominate the green fluorescence. With the thermal temperature increases to 210 °C, two evolution paths occur to G-CDs120. On the one hand, the intertwined polymer chains in G-CDs120 further go through aromatization and carbonization, forming carbogenic core with efficient π conjugated system. Although the temperature is high, many polymer chains or molecules are still not carbonized and remain connect to the carbon core [25]. It is important to note that G-CDs120 and R-CDs210 exhibit similar surface groups and chemical bonds. Therefore, the carbonized core plays significant roles for the red-shifted fluorescence of R-CDs210. On the other hand, a competitive nucleation growth may exist during the carbonization process of G-CDs120. Compared to R-CDs210, G-CDs210 have relatively lower carbonization degree and higher amount of oxygen element, as confirmed by XPS data. Therefore, we deduce that despite the portion transformed into R-CDs210, the rest part of G-CDs120 experience insufficient carbonization and excessive oxidation, giving rise to G-CDs210 with less carbonized core and more oxidized surface. Due to the formation of carbonized core, the PL center of G-CDs210 varies from surface sub-fluorophores into edge state of carbon core.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature triggered three typical CDs show varied optical property
ce:section.ce:para: Taking the distinct structures and compositions into account, we further thoroughly investigated the optical properties and some novel findings were exhibited. When exposed to a 365 nm UV light, G-CDs120 and G-CDs210 show typical aggregation-caused quenching in solid states while R-CDs210 powders emit strong deep red quench-resistant SSF (Fig. 5 a). Fig. 5b presents the PL spectrum of the R-CDs210 powder under different excitation wavelengths, displaying an excitation-independent PL feature with emission peak centered at around 660 nm. Such unique self-quenching resistant fluorescence in solid state is mainly ascribed to their special structure, which is discussed in detail later. Apart from the significant difference in SSF, the three CDs also show distinct PL properties in solution. G-CDs120 and G-CDs210 exhibit stable green emissions in most organic solvents, but when dispersed in polar protic solvents including ethanol, methanol and water, a red-shifted fluorescence from green to yellow is observed (Figs. S15–16). In principle, both dipole − dipole and hydrogen bonding (HB) interactions can contribute to the observed redshifts of fluorescence [45,46]. In order to confirm the reason of the red-shifted fluorescence, the two CDs were further dissolved in methanol, ethanol, n-propanol, 1-pentanol and 1-hexanol with successively decreased polarities. As shown in Figs. S15c and S16c, both G-CDs120 and G-CDs210 exhibit very similar fluorescence spectra in these alcohol solvents. As a result, it can be inferred that the observed redshifts of fluorescence should be ascribed to HB interactions instead of solvent polarity. In contrast, R-CDs210 exhibit high sensitivity to solvent polarity. Although the absorption spectra only display a slight bathochromic shift (Fig. 5c), R-CDs210 show a robust positive solvatochromic effect with fluorescence red shift from green (nonpolar Hexane, 508 nm) to far-red (polar pure water, 640 nm) region (~132 nm shift) upon the increase of solvent polarity (Fig. 5d). R-CDs210 are weakly emissive in polar protic solvents (Fig. 5e), particularly in pure water. We then measured the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs210 in the mixed solvents of 1,4-dioxane and water. The fluorescent intensity grows gradually with the increase of 1,4-dioxane volume content, meanwhile accompanied with blue-shifted emission from 640 nm to 565 nm (Fig. S17). These results suggest a strong intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) effect for R-CDs210 [47,48]. The absolute QY of R-CDs210 in these solvents are listed in Table S1, which dramatically decreases in polar protic solvents, showing same trend as the fluorescent intensity. As a result, in addition to the ICT effect, R-CDs210 can also be quenched in polar protic solvents by other mechanisms, particularly HB interactions with the solvents [48,49]. It is worth noting that the absolute QY of yellow fluorescence for R-CDs210 in DCM is as high as 73% under 480 nm excitation, which may serve as the highest value of yellow fluorescence for CDs to date.

On the basis of above results, CDs with different structures and compositions have been successfully realized by simply controlling the thermal temperature during one-pot solvothermal process. Benefiting from the rational designed polymer feature, R-CDs210 expectantly possess impressive optical properties of deep red SSF, robust solvatochromic effect and high QY. Actually, several groups have reported CDs with long wavelength SSF (Table S2). Interestingly, almost all these reported long wavelength SSF CDs show heavily blue-shifted fluorescence in diluted aqueous solution compare to their SSF, due to the typical RET effect, which usually occur when the distance of CDs particles is within Förster distance (R0) [14,42]. However, in our case, the diluted aqueous solution of R-CDs210 (0.01 mg mL−1) display red fluorescence with emission peak located at around 640 nm, showing a slight blue-shift compared to that of powders at 660 nm. The results indicate that R-CDS210 suffer from weak RET, despite the spectral overlap between their absorption and emission (Fig. 6 a). The fluorescence decay curves of R-CDs210 aqueous solution and powder further confirm above conclusion (Fig. 6b). The average lifetime of R-CDs210 powder is longer than that of the R-CDs210 aqueous solution, contrary to RET, which always leads to shorter average lifetime for powders because RET competes with radiative transition, and thus shortening the lifetime [50]. The weak RET in R-CDs210 powder should be ascribed to their unique polymer structure. Since R-CDs210 are comprised of carbonized cores and a shell of polymer chains, both of which synergistically contribute to the fluorescence of R-CDs210. In the aggregate, on the one hand, due to the shell of polymer chains, the distance between R-CDs210 is always greater than R0 (Fig. 6c), thus avoiding not only RET effect, but also π-π interaction process of carbonized cores, the major energy-transfer process responsible for self-quenching in solid state [14,15], which prevent R-CDs210 with aggregation state from quenching in some extent. On the other hand, owing to the protection from the network structure of polymer chains, the sub-fluorophores are unable to directly attach to each other, which is the main reason for SSF of R-CDs210 [51].

To gain deep insight into the PL mechanisms of R-CDs210, the fluorescent time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) and femtosecond transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy were employed. Four representative solvents including dichloromethane (DCM), DMF, ethanol and water were selected to conduct this investigation. As shown in Fig. 6d, the average lifetimes of R-CDs210 are measured to be 16.7, 12.17, 4.54 and 0.8 ns in DCM, DMF, ethanol and water, respectively, displaying a trend of reducing with the increase of solvent polarity. In general, the fluorescent lifetime and QY of most charge transfer compounds tend to diminish in polar solvents [52]. These results further confirm the charge transfer feature of R-CDs210. It should be noted that the PL decay curves of the R-CDs210 in all these solvents can be well fitted by mono exponential function, indicating that the radiative decay is extremely pure with a minimal nonradiative transition and a single, well-defined electronic transition is dominant for the fluorescence of the R-CDs210 [41,45]. Moreover, when probed at different emission wavelengths, the PL decay curves of R-CDs210 showed similar fluorescent lifetimes (Fig. 6e; Fig. S18, Tables S3–S6), further demonstrating that no energy transfer process competes with the radiative transition [51]. This result is further confirmed by the time-resolved emission spectra (TRES). It is clearly seen that the emission spectrum presents same wavelengths at 610 nm as the radiation decays from several to tens of ns (Fig. 6f; Fig. S19), revealing that there exists only one emission center in R-CDs210 without any energy transfer process. The overall data elucidate the single PL center of R-CDs210 and the fluorescence strongly depends on the charge transfer effect. Contrary to R-CDs210, the PL decay spectra of G-CDs120 and G-CDs210 reveal that HB interaction serves as the main reason responsible for their red-shifted fluorescence instead of solvent polarity (Figs. S20–22).

To further scrutinize the transfer and recombination process of the photogenerated charges, TA spectroscopy of R-CDs210 was measured under excitation of 365 nm. Fig. 6g shows the 2D TA spectra of R-CDs210 with probe wavelength in the range of 440–700 nm and scan delay time from 0.1 ps to 7 ns. The negative (dark purple) features in the range from 600 to 680 nm represent the stimulated emission (SE), while the relatively weaker positive (red and green) features from 510 to 570 nm correspond to the excited state absorption (ESA). Fig. 6h illustrates the TA spectra at different time delays. The negative peak of SE centered at 630 nm gradually increases within 1000 ps. The kinetic traces at different wavelengths as a function of delay time are also depicted in Fig. S23. In order to uncover the detailed relaxation channels of the excited carriers of R-CDs210, global fitting analyses were conducted based on the TA data. Fig. 6i shows the fitted decay associated difference spectra (DADS). Four distinctive decay components are obtained with fitted lifetimes determined to be 0.52 ps, 0.84 ps, 64 ps, and 12.2 ns, respectively. Based on the DADS, the relaxation channels of the excited carriers are revealed. After 365 nm excitation, the excited carriers firstly undergo Coulomb-induced thermalization within tens of femtoseconds, which is shorter than the instrumental response time (about 180 fs). The hot carriers then release excess energy through optical and acoustic phonon scattering and experience decay process of S2 state → S1 state within 0.52 ps, corresponding to internal conversion pathway. Subsequently, the charge redistribution or charge transfer process occurs rapidly (0.84 ps). During this process, the charge-separated transition state is formed, which further transforms into charge-separated state within 64 ps. Due to the existence of ions in charge-separated state, polar solvent can quench the energy level of ions, leading to high energy level converts to low energy level, and thus giving rise to red-shift fluorescence. The luminous surface states emit fluorescence via recombination of electrons and holes within the longest lifetime of 12.2 ns. The corresponding energy level diagram is depicted in Fig. S24. Intriguingly, the excited state relaxation channels obtained from the TA data directly demonstrate the strong ICT process of R-CDs210, consistent with previous results and analysis. As we know, the ICT effect is a typical feature of push–pull organic fluorescent compounds, and the most frequently employed structural motif for this effect is D-π-A [23,48]. Since R-CDs210 are comprised of carbonized core with efficient π conjugated system and polymer chains, as well as electron-donating groups (i.e., C–OH, C–N) and electron accepting groups (i.e., CO). These structures together form a specific D-π-A system, lay the foundation for the ICT character for R-CDs210.

ce:section.ce:section-title: R-CDs210 show high potential as LDs-specific probe
ce:section.ce:para: The remarkable optical properties of R-CDs210 have prompted us to exploit their practical applications. Due to the robust solvatochromic effect, R-CDs210 exhibit excellent solubility and strong emission in low polar hydrophobic media, indicating that R-CDs210 may serve as fluorescent probe to image lipid droplets (LDs). In order to preliminarily verify this feasibility, we investigated the photophysical behaviors of R-CDs210 in triglyceride, the main component of LDs, as well as in PBS buffer. As expected, R-CDs210 show good solubility in triglyceride and emit bright yellow fluorescence with large Stokes shifts>100 nm (Fig. 7 a and b). The absolute QY in oil is determined to be 51.62% (Fig. S25), which is suitable for bioimaging. Since R-CDs210 are weak emissive in PBS, thus an impressive fluorescence enhancement up to 140-fold is presented from PBS to oil, which can offer a favorable signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio (Fig. S26). According to the present results, R-CDs210 exhibit exceptional photophysical properties in oil media, appearing as suitable candidate to probe LDs in living cells.

Next, the cellular performance of R-CDs210 were tested. The cytotoxicity of R-CDs210 was firstly evaluated by a standard 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium (MTT) assay in Hela cells, HepG2 cells and MC3T3-E1 cells. As shown in Fig. S27, after incubating with R-CDs210 for 24 h, the cell viabilities for the three kinds of cells are over 80% even at the high concentration of R-CDs210 to 200 µg mL−1. Due to the high QY up to 51.62% in oil media, for the following biological imaging, 10 µg mL−1 of R-CDs210 was used and the cell viabilities are all higher than 90%. As a result, R-CDs210 are of low cytotoxicity and show great potential for biological applications. Then, cell imaging capability were further performed. After incubating HepG2 cells with R-CDs210 for 1 h (10 µg mL−1), bright yellow fluorescence can be observed within HepG2 cells under 488 nm laser excitation (Fig. S28), indicating superior cell permeability of R-CDs210. Moreover, the fluorescence in HepG2 cells presents point shape distribution, consistent with the morphology of LDs. To further confirm the precise location of R-CDs in live cells, we conducted co-localization experiments using BODIPY493/503, a commercial LDs dye. As shown in Fig. 7c, the fluorescence of R-CDs210 overlaps well with that of BODIPY493/503, with the corresponding Pearson’s colocalization coefficients (Rr) calculated to be 0.87, suggesting that R-CDs210 possess high specificity towards LDs. In addition, a three-dimensional (3D) image of R-CDs210 staining HepG2 cells is also displayed to further confirm the high specificity. Moreover, R-CDs210 also show high specificity towards LDs in carcinoma cell lines (Hela cells) and normal cell lines (MC3T3-E1 cells) (Fig. S29), suggesting general good LDs targeting efficiency of R-CDs210 in different cell lines. Photostability, as another significant criterion for evaluating a fluorescent probe, was checked by continuous laser irradiation. As illustrated in Fig. 7d, after continuous irradiation for 1 h, the fluorescence signal of R-CDs210 slightly decreases to 80% of its initial values. In comparison, the fluorescence intensity of BODIPY493/503 fades to 67% of its initial value under same conditions. The results reveal that R-CDs210 exhibit better resistance to photobleaching compared to the commercial dye. Besides, we further explored the specificity of R-CDs210 to LDs in living cells by costaining experiments of R-CDs210 with four cellular markers: DAPI for nuclei, Mitro Tracker Red for mitochondria, Lyso-Tracker Red for Lysosome, and CM-Dil for membrane. The results show that the dotted yellow fluorescence of R-CDs210 hardly merges with the fluorescence of these cellular markers (Fig. S30), implying that R-CDs210 can not target these organelles. Taking advantage of the bright yellow fluorescence of R-CDs210, multicolor cellular imaging can be realized by co-stain with DAPI, R-CDs210 and CM-Dil for nucleus, LDs and membrane, under excitation of 405, 488 and 543 nm, respectively (Fig. 7e). Taken together, R-CDs210 can be utilized as a robust imaging tool to monitor the LDs-related physiological process.

Subsequently, the ability of R-CDs210 to dynamically monitor LDs behavior was investigated because LDs accumulation has proved to be associated with many diseases. Since oleic acid (OA) can efficiently stimulate LDs generation in cells [53], we firstly explored R-CDs210 in monitoring LDs production and accumulation in cells. Hela cells were treated with OA for different incubation times and next stained with R-CDs210. Upon the increase of incubation time, more yellow fluorescence is observed in Hela cells (Fig. S31), revealing that R-CDs210 can monitor the OA-stimulated accumulation of LDs in living cells. The results further encourage us to investigate the application of R-CDs210 for real-time imaging of intracellular autophagy process of living cells. To induce autophagy, HepG2 cells were incubated in Hank’s balanced salt solution (HBSS), a kind of nutrient-free medium. Herein, mitochondrial dye MitoTracker Red was co-stained with R-CDs210 to provide deep insight into dynamic behavior of LDs during autophagy. As shown in Fig. 7f, with prolonged starvation, both the intensity and intracellular distribution of yellow fluorescence from R-CDs210 significantly increase, suggesting the generation of LDs during starvation-induced autophagy process. Moreover, an obvious accumulation of LDs around mitochondrial can be observed, consistent with the recent investigation of LDs catabolism, which is so-called starvation-induced “lipophagy” [47]. The results prove that R-CDs210 can serve as a promising fluorescent probe for real-time monitoring the dynamic variation of LDs within living cells.

On the basis of the impressive LDs imaging ability of R-CDs210 in cellular level, we moved further to study the capacities of R-CDs210 for tracking LDs in animal tissue and live zebrafish. Fatty liver is a LDs-related disease that is usually accompanied with the accumulation of LDs [54,55]. As shown in Fig. 8 a, R-CDs210 were utilized to stain liver tissue of mice fed in two different ways. For the general feeding one, LDs in liver tissue scatter uniformly. In sharp contrast, obvious LDs cluster can be observed in high-fat feeding mice (shown by red arrows in Fig. 8a). The results reveal that R-CDs210 can diagnose fatty liver. Subsequently, we applied R-CDs210 to visualize lipid-rich tissues in live zebrafish. After incubating with R-CDs210, strong yellow fluorescence is observed in zebrafish. Moreover, the distribution of the yellow fluorescence is similar to that of green fluorescence of BODIPY493/503, suggesting that R-CDs210 can efficiently track lipid-rich tissues in living zebrafish (Fig. 8b; Figs. S32–33). Note that during this process, no zebrafish showed any signs of acute toxicological responses. Thus, all these data elucidate that R-CDs210 have superior biocompatibility, high QY and impressive LDs-targeting ability. There is no doubt that R-CDs210 can be used as LDs-specific agent, showing high potential in biomedical applications.

In summary, we develop a simple synthetic strategy to prepare high-performance CDs by controlling the structure of carbon core and polymer cluster during a solvothermal approach. Totally polymer-like CDs with green emission (G-CDs120) formed at low temperature, with the increase of thermal temperature from 120 to 210 °C, can not only transform into a carbonized green CDs (G-CDs210), but also evolve into a new type of red emissive CDs (R-CDs210). Impressively, R-CDs210 exhibit remarkable PL properties, including deep red SSF (λem = 660 nm), strong positive solvatochromic effect with wide emission shift up to ~132 nm and high QYs (i.e., 73% for yellow fluorescence and 45% for red fluorescence). The unique polymer characteristics of R-CDs210, consisting of carbon core and intertwined polymer chains, are confirmed responsible for the observed PL properties. Specifically, the polymer chains of R-CDs210 can hinder their carbonized core and sub-fluorophores from direct contact, thus avoiding π-π interactions, yielding deep red SSF. Besides, the efficient π conjugated system, together with electron-donating groups and electron-accepting groups on the surface, may form specific D-π-A structure, account for the robust solvatochromic effect. Moreover, R-CDs210 exhibit excellent solubility and bright emission with QY up to 51.6% in oil with an impressive fluorescence enhancement up to 140-fold compared to aqueous media, coupled with good photostability and favorable biocompatibility, making them an efficient LDs-specific probe. Then, efficient in vitro and in vivo imaging of LDs in living cells, mice liver tissue and live zebrafish are demonstrated. This work will set the stage for developing highly performing CDs and expand LDs marker from organic dyes to fluorescent carbon dots with simple and low-cost synthetic procedure.

The multiple-color emissive CDs were synthesized by one-pot solvothermal method. Typically, O-phenylenediamine (2.0 g) and thiourea (0.5 g) were dissolved in 40 mL mixed solvents of DMF/water (3:1). Then the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at different temperature (120, 150, 170, 190 and 210 °C) for 10 h. After the reactor was cooled down to room temperature automatically, the obtained solution was firstly purified via 0.22 μm filter to remove large particles. Then, the crude products were purified via dry silica column chromatography using a mixture of dichloromethane and methanol as the eluent. It should be noted that during the process of silica column chromatography purification, pure dichloromethane was firstly used to separate the red fractions because these red fractions have relatively low polarity. Then, methanol was gradually mixed with dichloromethane in order to increase the polarity of the eluent. Since the green components have high polarity and meanwhile some impurities with high polarity still exist, the volume ratio of dichloromethane and methanol should be changed dynamically to adjust the polarity of eluent, aiming at efficiently separating and purifying the CDs. After collecting purified solution and removing the solvent through rotary evaporator, solid powders can be collected for further use.

HepG2 cells (1 × 105) were seeded in culture dishes (diameter: 35 mm dish with 14 mm bottom well) and incubated in 1 mL DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 for 24 h. After the removal of culture medium, R-CDs210 solution were added with final concentration of 10 μg mL−1 and incubated the cells solution for 1 h. After that, the R-CDS210 solution were removed and the cells were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) three times to discard non-specifically bound CDs. Subsequently, 1 μM BODIPY 493/503 was added and the cells solution was incubated for another 10 min. Then, the medium was removed and the cells were further washed with PBS for three times, and 1.0 mL of PBS was added into the dishes. Leica TCS-SP5 confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) was used to measure the cellular fluorescence images. The fluorescence image from R-CDs210 was recorded using excitation at 405 nm and that of BODIPY493/503 was recorded using excitation at 488 nm. The co-localization experiments of R-CDs210 with DAPI, Mitro Tracker Red, Lyso-Tracker Red and CM-Dil were similar to that of BODIPY493/503. DAPI: 5 μg mL−1, λex = 405 nm; Mitro Tracker Red: 2 μM, λex = 543 nm; Lyso Tracker Red: 2 μM, λex = 543 nm; CM-Dil: 2 μg mL−1, λex = 543 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring the dynamics of LDs during starvation
ce:section.ce:para: HepG2 cells were firstly seeded in culture dishes (diameter: 35 mm dish with 14 mm bottom well) and incubated with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2. Then, the medium was removed and the cells were further incubated in HBSS for different times (0–8 h). After that, the treated cells were stained with R-CDs210 (10 µg mL−1) for 1 h and Mito Tracker Deep Red (1 μM) for another 30 min. Next, the cells were washed with PBS for three times. Cells were immediately subjected to cell imaging by Leica TCS-SP5 confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) under 488 nm laser excitation.

All animal experiments were approved by the ethics committee of Sichuan University in accordance with the protocol. Four weeks old Kunming male mice were acquired from Chengdu Dashuo Laboratory Animal Center, China. To construct the mice fatty liver model, the mice were fed using high-fat diet for 28 days. Besides, during feeding, the mice were subcutaneously injected with 200 µL of tetrachloromethane and olive oil (the volume ratio of tetrachloromethane and olive oil is 0.3%) for three times, one week each time, aiming at destroying liver tissue and promoting liver fibrosis. Simultaneously, the mice fed with normal diet were used as control. The liver tissue slices of mice were directly removed from just killed mice raised in above two different ways of general feeding and high-fat feeding. For tissues imaging experiments, frozen tissue slices were stained with R-CDs210 (10 µg mL−1 for 0.5 h). Then, the stained tissue slices were imagined under a confocal microscope.

The zebrafish were obtained from Shanghai FishBio Co., Ltd. Live zebrafish were firstly placed in culture dishes (diameter: 35 mm dish with 14 mm bottom well) and incubated with ultrapure water at 28 ℃. Then, R-CDs210 (10 µg mL−1) and BODIPY493/503 (2 μM) were added in the culture dishes and incubate for another 2 h. Subsequently, the zebrafish were washed with ultrapure water for three times. The fluorescence images of zebrafish were measured using Leica TCS-SP5 confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) under 488 nm laser excitation. The All animal procedures were conducted in accordance with protocols approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of NIAID, NIH.
